posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Decanter have just posted a profile of Weingut Keller that I wrote for the magazine earlier this year on their website. Might be of interest to Keller fans.

Decanter – 7 May 17

Weingut Keller: Producer profile - Decanter

Klaus-Peter Keller took charge of Weingut Keller in 2001. His G-Max cuvée is the world’s most expensive dry Riesling, winning commensurate acclaim...

posts.name: John J
posts.cooked: Interesting that he did an internship at Armand Rousseau. One of my favorite Riesling producers.

posts.name: Khiem Le
posts.cooked:
William,

Very interesting profile.

I have yet to taste Keller!
It’s not easy to get your hands on German Riesling in France.

Khiem

posts.name: Ilkka |_ |_| mme
posts.cooked:
Khiem Le:

William,

Very interesting profile.

I have yet to taste Keller!
It’s not easy to get your hands on German Riesling in France.

Khiem

There are literally dozens of great German online retailers (with several of them selling Keller). Can you not use their services in France?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Khiem Le:

William,

Very interesting profile.

I have yet to taste Keller!
It’s not easy to get your hands on German Riesling in France.

Khiem

Thanks Khiem! I have a decent stash, though they are all young, so we can share some next time we meet. You should go visit KP though, he would love your book.

In the meantime, a good introduction to his oeuvre would be the 2015 “Limestone” or “Von der Fels” Rieslings.

posts.name: Ulrich Beier-Dahl
posts.cooked: Agree with William; the pair of Limestone Riesling and von der Fels are an excellent introduction to Keller wines, but it’s probably somewhat easier to get hold of 2016 of both versions. These are very good and worthy successors of 2015, which is not the case for all German producers in 2016, where I find many wines being a little too fat, lacking a touch of nerve and precision in an otherwise healthy vintage.

posts.name: Ulrich Beier-Dahl
posts.cooked: … and nice work on the producer profile, William. Nice to see some attention to German wines in Decanter.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Ulrich Beier-Dahl:

… and nice work on the producer profile, William. Nice to see some attention to German wines in Decanter.

Thanks Ulrich. I wish we could do more. There are so many interesting wines being produced.

posts.name: Khiem Le
posts.cooked:
IkkaL,

I try to avoid buying wines online, especially overseas. I am wary of the shipping/storage conditions.

I think I’ll just hit William Kelley for some! )

Khiem

posts.name: Lars Carlberg
posts.cooked:
Klaus Peter Keller might have an agent or a few stockists in France. You can send him an email of inquiry.

In the Saarland, Pinard de Picard carries a lot of Keller and has good storage conditions.
